reason: Windows and DOS ARE operating systems, so the stem's negative claim is False.
An OS is system software, not application software, so the statement is False.
Classic SDLC order: requirements analysis and specification precede design, coding, testing.
A programming language is defined by its syntax (form) and semantics (meaning).
A program is a detailed set of instructions telling the computer what to do; option B is an artificially narrow subset.
The ALU performs the elementary arithmetic and logic operations; the CPU is the containing unit.
printf/scanf are valid C library calls usable in C++; 'Reads' is not a C++ command.
Stack pointer holds the address of the top of the stack, not the data being worked on (that is the accumulator/registers).
CPU = ALU + control unit (+ registers); main memory is external to the CPU.
The CPU fetches and executes programs stored in memory; accumulator/register are sub-parts.
16 bits / 8 bits per byte = 2 bytes.
SRAM = Static RAM (static memory).
The three CPU components are ALU, control unit, registers; the I/O unit is outside the CPU.
Mbps = megabits per second (lowercase b); the stem's capitalisation is sloppy but the intended reading is bits.
CPU clock and memory directly determine compute speed; the hard disk is storage/IO and is the intended non-factor.
1 TB / 1 GB = 1024 (or 1000) and is dimensionless, not a byte count, so none of the listed 'Bytes' options is right.
WAN = Wide Area Network.
GUI = Graphical User Interface.
